article_title: Linux is finally dropping support for ancient hardware, and it's about time
core_thesis: The Linux kernel's removal of support for the i486 CPU (and other ancient hardware) is a necessary trade-off: maintaining compatibility with decades-old, unused hardware drains developer resources and hinders progress, while the few users of such hardware are unlikely to need or benefit from kernel updates. The article argues that legacy support should be retained only when there is a practical reason, not for the sake of pride in Linux's ability to run on anything.
detailed_summary: The article discusses Linux 7.1's removal of support for the i486 CPU (released in 1989) and argues that this is a positive step. The author, Simon Batt, contends that the pride associated with Linux running on any hardware is detrimental when it forces maintainers to support obsolete components that almost no one uses. He points out that users of i486 systems are unlikely to be downloading new kernels, and even if they are, the security risk is minimal because such machines are rarely connected to the internet. The article also references the recent removal of support for a niche cable type used only at a university decades ago. However, the author does not advocate for eliminating all legacy support; he acknowledges that some older hardware still has active, internet-connected users who benefit from security updates. The key is to cut support only for hardware that is truly obsolete and unused, freeing developers to focus on modern features and security.

entities.name: Simon Batt
entities.role: Author of the article, a technology writer with a background in computer science.

entities.name: Linus Torvalds
entities.role: Creator and maintainer of the Linux kernel, referenced as the decision-maker for hardware support.

entities.name: i486 CPU
entities.role: An Intel processor released in 1989, whose support was dropped in Linux 7.1.

entities.name: Linux 7.1
entities.role: The upcoming Linux kernel release that removes i486 support.

key_data_points: Linux 7.1 removes support for the i486 CPU, released in 1989, because maintaining compatibility is infeasible given negligible usage. (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: understanding kernel changes helps you anticipate attack surface reductions; older hardware removal means fewer legacy vectors to exploit.)

The article argues that the pride in 'Linux runs on anything' can harm the kernel by draining developer resources on obsolete hardware. (🟢 Relevant to your DevOps/Homelab: when building systems, prioritize modern hardware to avoid compatibility issues and benefit from performance improvements.)

Users of i486 systems are unlikely to download new kernels, and such machines are rarely internet-connected, making security updates unnecessary. (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: this reinforces that targeting ancient systems is low-value; focus on modern, connected infrastructure.)

The author supports retaining legacy support for hardware that still has active, internet-connected users who need security updates, but advocates cutting support for truly obsolete components like the i486 and a niche cable type removed in Linux 7.0.
conclusions: Dropping support for the i486 is a pragmatic decision that allows kernel developers to focus on modern hardware and security, rather than maintaining compatibility with a CPU that is nearly 40 years old and effectively unused. The article supports this by noting that the few i486 users are unlikely to update kernels or connect to the internet, so the removal has no real-world impact.

Legacy support should be retained only when there is a clear, practical reason—such as active users who rely on security updates. The author distinguishes between hardware that is still in use (e.g., older PCs with internet connectivity) and truly obsolete hardware like the i486, which should be allowed to 'pass on as a memory.'
personal_relevance_summary: This article is relevant to your interests in Linux, homelabbing, and cybersecurity. As a homelab enthusiast, you should be aware that future Linux kernels will drop support for very old hardware, so when building or upgrading your lab, prioritize modern CPUs to ensure compatibility and performance. For Red Teaming, the removal of legacy support reduces the attack surface of modern Linux systems, but also means that older, unsupported systems may become more vulnerable if left unpatched—though the article argues such systems are rarely connected. As a student, the article's reasoning about resource allocation can be applied to your own projects: focus effort where it yields the most benefit, and don't be afraid to drop outdated methods or tools.
extra_advice: Consider auditing your homelab hardware to ensure none of your systems rely on i486 or similarly obsolete components. If you have any ancient machines, either upgrade them or isolate them from your network to avoid security risks. For Red Teaming, keep an eye on kernel changelogs to identify removed features that might have been exploitable—sometimes old code paths hide vulnerabilities that are patched by removal rather than fixes.
